Q1: I organized my study routine around listening rather than reading whenever possible. Being 27 and working with limited resources, I've learned to maximize my time by downloading lecture audio and playing it during my commute or while doing household tasks. I'd dedicate about three hours daily to focused study, usually in the evenings after my other responsibilities. I found that setting up a consistent audio-based routine helped me retain information better than traditional reading methods.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I tried to maintain some consistency with my evening study blocks, but honestly, when assignments were due, I'd intensify my efforts significantly. Some weeks I'd barely touch the materials if nothing was pressing, then I'd have marathon sessions when exams approached. Being a bit older, I've learned to trust my ability to perform under pressure even if my attendance wasn't perfect.
Q3: I usually logged into the LMS when I needed to check for new announcements or upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to participate in discussions since I found those valuable for hearing different perspectives. If I knew a new lecture video was posted, that would prompt me to get online and download it for later listening. Honestly, the notification emails were my biggest trigger for logging in.
Q4: After the first few weeks, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine alongside my coffee. I'd quickly scan for any updates or new materials while planning my day. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me be more intentional about this—I started approaching the LMS check as a calm, focused activity rather than an anxious scramble. It became almost meditative to review what needed attention each day.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as an auditory learner—I watched over 200 minutes of content and often re-listened to complex sections. The discussion forums were surprisingly valuable too; reading and responding to my peers helped me process concepts in new ways. I also appreciated any audio resources or podcasts the instructors included. These formats matched how my brain naturally absorbs information.
Q6: I'll admit I often skimmed or ignored lengthy reading PDFs and text-heavy resources. With my auditory learning style, dense written materials just don't stick as well for me. I also delayed some of the supplementary readings when they weren't directly tied to assignments. It wasn't that I thought they were useless—I just had to prioritize given my limited time and resources.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to exam content or my actual understanding of the subject. I valued anything I could engage with through listening or discussion. If an activity helped me make connections between concepts or gave me something to think about during my day, that felt meaningful. Busy work without clear purpose was what I tried to avoid.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford given my resource constraints and responsibilities. Required activities got my attention first, always. But when optional activities were discussion-based or audio content, I was more likely to engage with them because they fit my learning style. I think I approached optional work with curiosity rather than obligation, which sometimes made it more enjoyable.
Q9: Absolutely—a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen during commutes, while cooking, even while exercising. I took handwritten notes while listening and would review those notes repeatedly. I also used free external resources like YouTube explanations and podcasts on related topics. My actual learning extended far beyond what any system could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deeply engaged with course concepts while appearing completely inactive online. I'd spend hours with my audio notes, talking through problems out loud to myself, or discussing concepts with friends who weren't in the course. Some of my best exam preparation happened while walking around my neighborhood listening to recorded lectures. None of that showed up anywhere.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture once, download or take detailed audio notes, and then work with that material offline for days or weeks. My 95% exam score came largely from this repeated offline engagement with materials I'd only accessed once online. I learned early in my academic career that I retain more through repeated listening than through repeated online access.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became harder, usually when financial stress peaked or when I had competing work responsibilities. My low attendance rate reflects times when I simply couldn't be physically or mentally present. The mindfulness intervention actually helped during one particularly stressful period—it gave me tools to manage my anxiety and re-engage when I felt overwhelmed. I was surprised by how effective those techniques were.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage patterns. When I was exhausted, I'd default to passive listening rather than active participation in discussions. My assignment completion rate suffered when work demands increased. I learned to be strategic—focusing my limited energy on high-impact activities like exam preparation rather than spreading myself thin across everything.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was elsewhere, especially late at night when I was tired. I'd scroll through materials without really processing them. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was stressed about other things. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they showed as activity.
Q15: I postponed several assignments even knowing they were important, which is reflected in my completion rate. Sometimes it was resource-related—I didn't have access to materials I needed or the quiet space to focus. Other times it was about energy management; I knew I'd do better work after rest than forcing myself through exhaustion. My resilience isn't always the highest, so I had to be strategic about when to push and when to wait.
Q16: Deadlines were major drivers of my LMS activity. I'd see a spike in my engagement as due dates approached. I'm not proud of it, but I definitely relied on deadline pressure to motivate intensive study sessions. The structure of having clear deadlines actually helped me manage my time across seven online courses. Without them, I might have drifted too much.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, though I sometimes had to wait longer than I'd like to receive it. When I got positive feedback on discussion posts, it motivated me to participate more. Critical feedback on assignments helped me adjust my approach for exams. I tried to seek out feedback through peer interactions too, since those were more immediate.
Q18: The course structure worked reasonably well for my situation. Having materials available for download meant I could work around my schedule and resource limitations. The mix of videos, discussions, and assignments gave me multiple ways to engage. I appreciated that the course didn't require constant synchronous attendance, which would have been impossible for me.
Q19: Audio and video content made engagement much easier for me. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged meaningful interaction. What made it harder was when materials were only available in text-heavy formats or when activities required resources I didn't have easy access to. The flexibility of the course design was generally a strength.
Q20: I think high activity with low submission could reflect students who are genuinely trying to learn but struggling with the actual production of work. Maybe they're exploring, trying to understand, but hitting barriers when it comes to assignments. It could also be students dealing with perfectionism or anxiety about submitting imperfect work. Or simply people who engage deeply with content but have external circumstances preventing completion.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience. My exam score was 95% despite moderate LMS activity because so much of my learning happened offline. I think this pattern is common among experienced learners who've developed efficient study strategies. We know how to extract what we need from materials without extensive online interaction. The LMS activity doesn't capture the depth of offline engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They capture participation in discussions and submission patterns. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline study time, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They can't measure understanding, only activity. For auditory learners like me, most meaningful learning is invisible to these systems.
Q23: Being engaged means actively wrestling with ideas, not just passively consuming content. It means thinking about course concepts during my daily life, discussing them with others, and connecting them to what I already know. For me, engagement is largely internal and auditory—replaying lectures in my mind, talking through problems. It's about genuine curiosity and investment in understanding, regardless of what shows up in any tracking system.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while commuting or doing chores. It wouldn't capture my conversations with friends about course concepts or my handwritten notes that I reviewed repeatedly. It can't measure how the mindfulness intervention changed my relationship with stress and studying. My journey as a 27-year-old returning student with limited resources but high determination—that story doesn't fit in activity logs.
